title: My Conversation with the excellent Joel Mokyr - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Joel Mokyr argues the Europe-China Great Divergence is best measured by a widening technological and scientific-capability gap visible from the Renaissance, not by living standards, and rooted in European aggressiveness and outward-looking entrepreneurship versus Chinese passivity.

insights.claim: Mokyr contends the Europe-China divergence is better measured by the technological gap, visible from the Renaissance, than by living-standards comparisons, which he is skeptical can be accurately measured.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Reframes long-run growth analysis around innovation capacity rather than consumption metrics, relevant to how investors weigh technological capability vs GDP-per-capita in assessing national trajectories.
insights.quote: In the end, what you see by the 1830s and 1840s, you see that the technological gap is huge, in some ways much larger than the living standards gap.
insights.locator: Why does it take so long

insights.claim: By the First Opium War a single English ship could blow the Chinese empire to pieces, illustrating how large the technological gap had grown even though Chinese food production and famine rates were comparable to Europe's.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Military-technological capability, not subsistence, determined geopolitical dominance historically, an analogy for how a capability gap can suddenly translate into strategic collapse.
insights.locator: When you see what happens during the First Opium War

insights.claim: Mokyr characterizes Europeans as more aggressive, outward-looking entrepreneurs who acted as global agents of change, while the Chinese passively accepted or rejected imported crops and techniques rather than driving change.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A culture-of-agency thesis for long-run outperformance; frames entrepreneurial initiative as the differentiating variable between societies.
insights.quote: Europeans are more aggressive. They are more outward-looking.
insights.locator: They are accepting the stuff that the Europeans did

insights.claim: Part of European growth came from copying Chinese and Indian imports; the Industrial Revolution partly consisted of Europeans building institutions to mimic imported goods like Chinaware and cotton, then displacing those imports once matched.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Import-substitution-through-imitation is a recurring path to industrial catch-up, a template echoed in modern emerging-market industrialization.
insights.locator: Now, keep in mind, of course

insights.claim: Analysis of Needham's Science and Civilisation in China found almost no innovation occurring after 1400, with complete stagnation and even loss of prior capabilities like the sophisticated 11th-century clocks.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Technological capability can regress, not just plateau; a caution that accumulated know-how is perishable without institutions sustaining it.
insights.quote: almost nothing that Needham pointed out as an innovation happens after 1400
insights.locator: Somebody actually went out and looked at Joseph Needham

insights.claim: By 1700 Europe was on the verge of transforming the understanding of nature through a broad body of scientific work beyond Newton and Galileo, with no parallel in a China that had high literacy and organized education but did not continue its scientific forays.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: High literacy and education alone did not produce sustained innovation in China, suggesting institutional and cultural drivers matter more than human-capital stocks.
insights.locator: Certainly, by 1700, Europe is on the verge
